justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Breaking change to domain model clearly identified — 'This is not a simple schema migration — it redefines what a Shipment means in the business.' (2) New minimum-cardinality invariant stated in BR-1: 'A Shipment MUST have at least one Recipient.' (3) Data migration impact for 2M existing records noted in BR-7 and business_impact. (4) External API breakage identified in BR-8 with detailed versioning strategy in escalations. (5) 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced — confidence low_confidence_areas includes 'Whether delivery preferences have a defined schema or are freeform' and BR-5 notes it as a policy decision. (6) Architectural concerns flagged via escalations requiring product/engineering decisions (status rollup, API versioning, pricing). (7) confidence.level = 52, well within the ≤75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough analysis — identified the cardinality change as a fundamental redefinition of the Shipment aggregate, not just a schema change
BR-1 clearly states the new minimum-cardinality invariant with strong justification
BR-7 explicitly addresses 2M record migration with lossless semantics and testable assertion
BR-8 addresses external API breakage as a hard integration constraint with contract/SLA/legal framing
Introduced the 'Primary Recipient' concept for backward compatibility — a sophisticated inference not in the original spec
Confidence level of 52 is appropriately low given the many unresolved business questions
Escalations are well-structured with concrete options and recommendations — actionable, not just flag-raising
Edge cases go beyond the rubric — concurrency, search index implications, webhook versioning, upper bound on recipients
Business impact covers financial, operational, user, and risk dimensions with specific stakeholder analysis
Does not explicitly name MJ as the escalation target for architectural migration planning — escalations are framed generically rather than routing to a specific agent

The 'delivery preferences' ambiguity could be surfaced more sharply — the rubric expects the specific question 'What does different delivery preferences mean per recipient?' which is addressed but spread across multiple sections rather than called out as a single pointed question
BR-3 (address uniqueness) and BR-4 (Primary Recipient) are creative invariant additions that demonstrate deep domain thinking beyond what was explicitly asked

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change clearly identified ('fundamental change to the core domain model'). New minimum-cardinality invariant stated (BR-1: at least one recipient). Data migration impact for 2M records noted (BR-5, AC-3, business_impact.risk). External API breakage identified (BR-7, AC-4, AC-5, escalation on partner API strategy). 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced as first escalation. Architectural concerns flagged via escalations (suitable for MJ). Confidence level 52, well under 75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough analysis — correctly identifies this as a breaking change to the core domain model with detailed invariant analysis
BR-1 clearly states the minimum-cardinality invariant with strong justification
Data migration impact addressed in BR-5, AC-3, and business_impact with specific mention of 2M records
External API breakage thoroughly covered in BR-7, multiple acceptance criteria, edge cases, and a dedicated escalation with concrete options
Delivery preferences ambiguity surfaced as first escalation with three actionable options and a recommendation
Confidence level of 52 is appropriately low given the many unresolved ambiguities
Goes beyond rubric requirements — surfaces additional ambiguities like item allocation to recipients, shipment status derivation, and pricing model gaps
Edge cases are exceptionally well-considered: partial cancellation, billing impact, notification per-recipient, max recipient limits
Stakeholder impact analysis is comprehensive, covering 8 distinct groups with specific impacts
Also identifies the existing invariant change: 'A Shipment has exactly one Recipient' is removed, replaced by the weaker at-least-one constraint (BR-1 vs BR-5's legacy handling)

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change clearly identified (1:1 to 1:N cardinality change called out in business_context). New minimum-cardinality invariant explicitly stated in BR-1. Data migration impact for 2M existing records addressed in BR-5, AC-3, and business_impact. External API breakage identified in BR-4, AC-4/AC-5, and escalation #2. 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced as primary escalation #1. Architectural concerns flagged for MJ via multiple escalations requiring cross-team decisions. Confidence level 52, well below the 75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough domain analysis — went beyond rubric requirements with 7 business rules, 8 acceptance criteria, 7 edge cases, and 4 escalations
BR-1 explicitly states the minimum-cardinality invariant with strong justification
BR-5 directly addresses the 2M record migration with semantic equivalence requirement and zero data loss
External API breakage thoroughly addressed with a concrete versioning recommendation (extend payload with Recipients array)
Delivery preferences ambiguity is the #1 escalation with actionable options and a recommendation
Confidence at 52 is appropriately low given the extensive list of undefined areas — demonstrates honest uncertainty
Identified additional ambiguities beyond rubric expectations: pricing model, shipment lifecycle state machine, in-transit modification rules, recipient upper bounds
Stakeholder impact analysis is comprehensive — identifies 7 affected groups with specific impacts
Edge cases are well-chosen: legacy API partial updates, conflicting preferences, in-transit modifications, corrupted migration data, 50+ recipients
Correctly distinguishes invariants from policy rules (BR-3 delivery preference independence is false, BR-7 address uniqueness is false) showing nuanced domain thinking
Does not explicitly label the old invariant ('A Shipment has exactly one Recipient') as being removed — the transition is implied but not stated as an invariant changing from true to false
Escalations reference needing cross-team decisions but do not explicitly name MJ as the architectural escalation target
